steps.title: Identify the defining claim
steps.content:
The passage opens by stating it was “happy” for Rev. Barton that he did not overhear the neighbors' judgment. The narrator then poses a rhetorical question: would any of us want to compare our self-image with what others think of us?

This directly signals the passage's main idea—ignorance of how others perceive you is preferable to knowing.

steps.title: Apply to the choices
steps.content:
Choice D—”People are better off not knowing about the discrepancy between their own self-image and what others think of them”—directly states the main idea.

The metaphor reinforces it: we sustain ourselves through “conceit” (self-delusions); without these illusions, our capacity for good vanishes.

Choices A, B, and C discuss fixation on approval, generosity obstacles, and criticism avoidance—none of which the passage addresses.

That matches choice D — D is correct.
